title: Friends And Family

jokes.text: Johnny says 'this is so unnecessary' as the family prepares to give him a gift
jokes.setup: Johnny is being presented with a surprise gift from his family
jokes.punchline: His first reaction is to dismiss it as unnecessary before even seeing it — classic self-deprecating deflection that signals his anxious nature
jokes.explanation: Gentle opener that establishes Johnny's self-effacing character. Functional but not special — it's the kind of line any anxious father character might say.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I want to be very clear that I had nothing to do with this.

jokes.text: David refuses to attach his name to a gift he had no hand in selecting
jokes.setup: The family is presenting Johnny with a group gift
jokes.punchline: David immediately distances himself from it on aesthetic/credit grounds before anyone even knows what it is
jokes.explanation: Pure David Rose — his brand of fastidious self-distancing is perfectly in character. The joke lands because it reveals his vanity before we even know the stakes. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, you all look absolutely miserable.

jokes.text: Johnny says 'I was talking to my wife' when Roland answers Moira's question about where they found the portrait
jokes.setup: Moira asks where they found 'this,' clearly directing the question at Johnny
jokes.punchline: Roland answers enthusiastically, prompting Johnny to cut him off with quiet exasperation
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Johnny's weary correction of Roland's social obliviousness. Well-executed economy — four words carry the whole dynamic between these characters.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, I was in a portrait session? I don't remember that at all.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, you were in rehab during the sitting.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that would explain it.

jokes.text: Roland looks at the family portrait and says 'You all look like cartoons.'
jokes.setup: The Rose family gathers to admire their recovered portrait, expecting admiration
jokes.punchline: Roland delivers his honest, blunt aesthetic assessment: they look like cartoons
jokes.explanation: Roland's blunt sincerity is perfectly calibrated. 'You all look like cartoons' is economical and devastating — it's the kind of thing only Roland would say aloud, and it works because it's probably accurate given the described extravagance of the portrait.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I was not in rehab. I was visiting someone in rehab.

jokes.text: Alexis reveals she wasn't in the portrait because she was 'in rehab' — then Moira corrects: 'visiting Stavros,' then Johnny reveals her face was painted onto the assistant's body
jokes.setup: Alexis says she doesn't remember posing for the portrait
jokes.punchline: Three-beat revelation: (1) she was in rehab, (2) actually visiting someone in rehab, (3) her face was painted onto his assistant's body anyway — escalating absurdity
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the episode's first act. Three-beat escalation with each revelation more absurd than the last: Alexis's casual mention of rehab, Moira's correction revealing even more chaotic backstory, then Johnny's jaw-dropping admission that he had Alexis's face painted onto his assistant's body — and nobody treats this as remotely unusual. It's perfect Schitt's Creek: dark backstory delivered with cheerful nonchalance. Each character's response is completely in-character. Justifies 8+ on all axes.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, the assistant's proportions were so generous, it actually made you look rather svelte by comparison.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Hm. That's why you look so good.' — responding to learning her face was painted on the assistant's body
jokes.setup: Johnny reveals Alexis's face was on his assistant's body in the portrait
jokes.punchline: Alexis's only response is a pleased observation that this explains why she looks good — pure vanity, zero outrage
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on joke #5. In five words, this line encapsulates everything about Alexis Rose: her vanity, her lack of self-awareness, and her breezy acceptance of the absurd. It's a callback that earns its own score because it transforms the escalation into something even funnier by being the most wrong possible response. Quotable, character-defining, economical. Easily one of the best lines in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Roland, I need help moving this portrait.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'll do it for fifty bucks.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That seems reasonable.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Actually, make it a hundred.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? You just said fifty.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I looked at it again. Two hundred.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Roland!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Three hundred. Final offer. And I'm leaving.

jokes.text: Roland repeatedly raises his price offer to help carry the portrait inside, going from $20 to $30 to $40 as David keeps getting called and not hearing
jokes.setup: The portrait needs to be brought inside; Roland offers to help for $20
jokes.punchline: Each time David is called and doesn't respond, Roland raises his price, cheerfully willing to keep going all day
jokes.explanation: The escalating bribe is a tight little running gag that works because Roland's logic is perfectly backward — he's offering more money to help a neighbor, which only makes sense for Roland. 'I can keep going up, can play this game all day, pal' is a great button.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why is this portrait so... imposing?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, it's a large painting of an intimidating man. Both things can be true.

jokes.text: David can't tell if the room is very small or the portrait is very big — 'I think it's quite possible that both are true'
jokes.setup: The enormous family portrait has been brought into the already tiny motel room
jokes.punchline: Moira's confirmation that both are true — the room is small AND the portrait is enormous — while treating this as a perfectly normal situation
jokes.explanation: The visual comedy of the absurdly large portrait in the tiny motel room is doing a lot of work here. Moira's matter-of-fact confirmation is perfectly deadpan. The visual gag is stronger than the dialogue.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We could move some of Moira's wigs. They'd make excellent furniture.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Excuse me?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, they're quite sturdy. We could stack them, use them as a side table.

jokes.text: Johnny struggling to compliment the portrait, pausing for an uncomfortably long beat before landing on 'enormous strength of this family'
jokes.setup: Moira says she wanted to do something thoughtful; Johnny tries to respond with gratitude
jokes.punchline: A long pause as Johnny clearly struggles to find anything nice to say about the portrait, finally landing on the hilariously generic 'enormous strength'
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the pause — Johnny visibly searching for something genuine to say and landing on vague platitude. The beat '[02:26] enormous strength of this family' after a gap is the punchline. Warm and very Johnny Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: This portrait really captures the strength of our family, the unity we've always shared.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, we're living in a motel.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, this portrait was painted when we had considerably more...

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: ...everything.

jokes.text: David refuses to share his juice, explaining he doesn't 'normally share beverages with people'
jokes.setup: Patrick points out David took juice from the shared fridge without paying
jokes.punchline: David's response pivots entirely — instead of addressing the fairness of the juice, he reveals his general policy against sharing beverages
jokes.explanation: Efficient character comedy — David's fastidiousness about sharing beverages is both funny and entirely believable for him. The deflection from the actual accusation is clever structural writing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can't share a juice box with you. Do you know how many germs are on the opening?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Yes, David. Thousands. Millions, even. All plotting to destroy you through a juice box.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'Really. That is shocking news.' — responding to David's beverage-sharing policy
jokes.setup: David declares he doesn't normally share beverages
jokes.punchline: Patrick's bone-dry sarcasm: 'That is shocking news' — the opposite of surprised
jokes.explanation: Patrick's deadpan is perfectly calibrated. The joke works because of how unsurprised he is — he already knows David completely. Economy is excellent: maximum information in four words.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I don't share beverages because I have a very strict hierarchy of mouth cleanliness, and frankly, most people fall into the 'questionable' category.

jokes.text: David's 'clean mouth' vs. 'sloppy mouth' theory for beverage sharing
jokes.setup: David is deciding whether to share his juice with Patrick
jokes.punchline: David has a personal taxonomy of mouths — 'clean' vs. 'sloppy' — and uses it to evaluate Patrick's eligibility as a juice-sharer
jokes.explanation: The 'clean mouth/sloppy mouth' distinction is a delightfully specific piece of absurdist logic that feels exactly like David Rose — he has invented an entire aesthetic framework for something no one has ever thought about. Highly quotable and original. The callback at 04:17 where he withholds the juice ('you've got a sloppy mouth') elevates both jokes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A soft launch is a strategic business decision. It's how Gwyneth Paltrow built Goop.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And look at her now. She's a billionaire.

jokes.text: David's Gwyneth Paltrow/goop soft launch justification
jokes.setup: Patrick pushes back on the soft launch idea, suggesting David doesn't think people will show up
jokes.punchline: David defends the strategy by citing Gwyneth Paltrow soft-launching the goop newsletter as evidence of its effectiveness
jokes.explanation: David citing Gwyneth Paltrow as a business role model is perfectly in character — it's the kind of celebrity-lifestyle-brand reasoning that defines his worldview. Patrick's 'I have no idea what you're talking about' is the perfect straight-man button.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, that's my juice.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, I have a very small mouth.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I know you do.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: So I need to sip things slowly.

jokes.text: David withholds the juice at the end of the scene: 'No. You've got a sloppy mouth.'
jokes.setup: David has been debating sharing his juice; scene is wrapping up with Patrick asking for the juice back
jokes.punchline: David uses his own earlier 'sloppy mouth' taxonomy against Patrick, now judging him as having a sloppy mouth
jokes.explanation: The callback lands beautifully because the setup was so specific and absurd. Patrick asking for 'his' juice back only to be denied using the very framework David invented earlier is structurally tight and very funny. It completes the bit perfectly. The flip in whose juice it actually is gets a final twist here too.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So your patient said that he wanted to make sure that you knew that he was very satisfied with his legal representation.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, I'm a veterinarian.

jokes.text: Alexis avoids checking her grades by avoiding the computer entirely, calling it 'not really an option' for herself
jokes.setup: Ted notices Alexis isn't taking messages; she explains she's avoiding the computer because grades are posted
jokes.punchline: Ted points out that avoiding the computer 'is not really an option' — Alexis treating it as a valid strategy she's chosen
jokes.explanation: Functional character comedy — Alexis's avoidance strategy is believable but the joke isn't particularly sharp. Sets up the grade reveal well.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, so there was definitely a message. I'm pretty sure it was a person. They said words.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It was definitely about something. Something that happened, or will happen, or... exists.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And they sounded like they wanted me to do something about it. Or know about it. Or they just... called.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Honestly, it could have been a wrong number. Or the right number. There was definitely a phone involved.

jokes.text: Alexis initially thinks the class averages (60 and 65) are her grades and celebrates them
jokes.setup: Alexis checks her grades and screams that she passed with a 60 and a 65
jokes.punchline: Ted corrects her: those are the CLASS AVERAGES. She actually got a 63 and a 68, which are indeed passing — causing double celebration
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed two-beat joke. First laugh: Alexis celebrating scores that are barely passing. Second and bigger laugh: Ted's correction reveals those were the AVERAGES, and her actual scores (still barely passing) prompt a second, bigger celebration. The misdirection and escalation work perfectly together. It reveals Alexis's character while being genuinely surprising. Strong candidate for standout moment.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I got a 92 in Biology!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: That's... that's the class average, Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, yeah, and I'm in the class, so...

jokes.text: Ted's awkward 'work-o' exit after the grade celebration — '...will go to get back to... work-o'
jokes.setup: The grade celebration has just happened; there's romantic tension between Ted and Alexis; Ted tries to exit normally
jokes.punchline: Ted's stumbling sentence ending in the made-up 'work-o' — a verbal tic that signals the underlying awkwardness between them
jokes.explanation: 'Work-o' is a small, charming verbal stumble that becomes a running joke across this episode. It signals Ted's flustered state without over-explaining it.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I should probably get going. It's getting late and I have a lot of work-o tomorrow.

jokes.text: David walks in on the giant portrait and asks 'Did it grow?'
jokes.setup: David enters the motel room where the enormous family portrait now dominates the space
jokes.punchline: His first question is whether the portrait has physically grown since he last saw it
jokes.explanation: Three words, maximum economy. 'Did it grow?' perfectly captures the joke: the portrait is so absurdly large that size itself becomes elastic. A quick, punchy visual gag line.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why is this portrait so large? It's completely disproportionate to this room.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's a statement piece, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a statement that we have no taste and no spatial awareness.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I tried. The sheets aren't big enough.' — trying to cover the portrait
jokes.setup: David suggests covering the portrait with something
jokes.punchline: Johnny has already tried; even full bedsheets can't cover it
jokes.explanation: Good economy — the image of Johnny attempting to drape bedsheets over a portrait so large they don't reach is a great visual implied entirely through dialogue. Reinforces the absurdist scale of the portrait gag.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A soft launch is when you introduce a product or service to a limited audience first, to test the market before a full launch.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That sounds smart.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It is. Which is why I'm soft launching my irritation with you into a full launch of anger.

jokes.text: David's business-speak slip: 'It's always better to go under... Go under? David, if the business is foundering—'
jokes.setup: David is trying to explain the soft launch strategy using the phrase 'better to go under than over on expectations'
jokes.punchline: He says 'go under' which sounds like business failure; Moira panics; David scrambles to clarify the idiom while digging deeper
jokes.explanation: The verbal misfire creates a satisfying cringe-comedy moment as David's family momentarily thinks his business is failing before it's even opened. Moira's alarm and Johnny's gentle 'I don't know if that's true, son' are perfectly calibrated responses.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: When we opened a video store, we'd spare no expense on the grand opening. We had fireworks, we had pyrotechnics, we had a blimp that said 'Johnny Rose's House of VHS.'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That seems excessive for a video store.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, that's showmanship, David. You have to make an impression. People remember spectacle.

jokes.text: Johnny's discount math: 'What if your mother and I go in and buy something together? Does that mean we get a 50 percent discount?'
jokes.setup: David offers a 25% friends-and-family discount; Johnny notes friends get the same discount as family
jokes.punchline: Johnny's 'logical' extension: if two family members buy together, does the discount stack to 50%? David's response — 'Why would you want to spend less money at my store?' — is the real button
jokes.explanation: Johnny's negotiating logic is perfectly absurd — he's genuinely trying to get a deal from his own son's new business. David's horrified response ('why would you want to spend LESS money at my store?') flips the perspective brilliantly. Good structural setup/punchline work.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So you're telling me that if I bring in my entire family, we each get a discount?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, that's how it works.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So if I bring in David, Alexis, and you, that's three discounts. But what if David brings Stevie, and Alexis brings Ted? That's five more people.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, that's not how this works.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm onto something. If everyone brings someone, and each person gets a discount, eventually we're not paying for anything. We'll owe them money.

jokes.text: Moira calls the soft launch 'a modest little vigil you're hosting'
jokes.setup: David has been explaining the soft launch concept
jokes.punchline: Moira's word choice — 'vigil' — suggests something mournful and funereal rather than a business opening
jokes.explanation: Moira's vocabulary is always precise and always devastating. 'Vigil' is a perfect word choice — it implies mourning and death-watch rather than commerce, which is exactly what she thinks of the soft launch. Very character-specific and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it's less of an opening and more of a vigil.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Rarely has that been a recipe for success' — responding to David saying 'the smaller the better'
jokes.setup: David says he wants to keep the launch small
jokes.punchline: Moira's magisterial, historically sweeping dismissal: 'Rarely has that been a recipe for success'
jokes.explanation: Moira speaking as though she's commenting on the sweep of human history about a store's launch party is a great character beat. The pomposity of her delivery makes a fairly simple dismissal feel grand.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's fine, Ted. Adults kiss all the time. It's just a friendly thing.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Right, like how you kiss your brother.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, not like that.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Or your father.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, Ted.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Your mother?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Why are you like this?

jokes.text: Johnny and Moira call out 'We love you! Keep reaching, son!' as David leaves in exasperation
jokes.setup: David has just stormed out of the room after receiving only discouragement about his launch
jokes.punchline: Johnny and Moira pivot instantly to enthusiastic, generic parental encouragement — shouted at the door
jokes.explanation: 'Keep reaching, son!' is the funniest part — the catchphrase-level encouragement lobbed at a retreating adult child who just received no useful support. The Roses' brand of loving-but-oblivious parenting in miniature.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I love a good deal. I mean, who doesn't? But I think I might have a problem.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I've been buying vacuums. Like, a lot of vacuums. I don't even use them. I just... I see them on sale and I buy them.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I have seventeen vacuums in my room right now.

jokes.text: Ted and Alexis's 'adult kiss' conversation — rationalized as 'like when your parents kiss their friends on the mouth'
jokes.setup: Ted and Alexis need to address a kiss that happened between them while both claiming it wasn't a big deal
jokes.punchline: Their mutual rationalization devolves into comparing it to parents kissing friends on the mouth — an increasingly uncomfortable and unconvincing analogy
jokes.explanation: The increasingly absurd rationalization — 'like when your parents kiss their friends... on the mouth' — is classic cringe comedy. Each step away from acknowledging the real feelings makes them more obvious. The 'yes, like kissing your parents' agreement from Alexis is the perfect mortified button.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I should probably get going. It's been... ew, david!

jokes.text: Ted reveals he deleted Groupon because 'three vacuums is enough'
jokes.setup: Ted is explaining why discounts appeal to him so much
jokes.punchline: He deleted the app after accumulating three vacuums — a very specific, very funny domestic detail
jokes.explanation: 'Three vacuums is enough' is a delightfully specific piece of character-building. The number three is exactly right — one is normal, two is a problem, three is a pattern requiring intervention. Pure economy and specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, family portraits are a very important part of any business. They establish trust, they show stability, they demonstrate that you have people who care about you.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: It's just a very large portrait of your face.

jokes.text: Ted trips/stumbles as he leaves the room, Alexis reacts with 'Watch out! Oh. Oh!'
jokes.setup: Ted is departing after the awkward kiss conversation
jokes.punchline: He stumbles on the way out — physical comedy that undercuts the moment and mirrors his emotional awkwardness
jokes.explanation: Physical punctuation to the scene. The stumble works as a button but it's not the show's strongest physical gag — more of a reliable sitcom fallback. Gets credit for being character-appropriate (Ted's earnest clumsiness) but it's not especially crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: They're going to notice it immediately.

jokes.text: Stevie's deadpan: 'Oh. That reminds me, I've been meaning to have my family portrait airlifted in...'
jokes.setup: Johnny explains that family businesses often hang portraits of the family in the workplace, having just installed the enormous Rose portrait in the motel
jokes.punchline: Stevie's bone-dry mock-agreement: she's 'been meaning' to do the same — the absurdity of the motel having a giant family portrait played completely straight
jokes.explanation: Stevie's dry wit at its finest. 'Airlifted in' is the perfect detail — it implies the scale of the portrait and gently mocks Johnny's explanation simultaneously. The word 'airlifted' does all the work. Economical, character-perfect, and a great callback to the portrait's established enormity. Justifies its 8.0 score through originality of the specific word choice and Stevie's unmistakable voice.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, it's a very niche market. People who want a portrait of someone they don't know, painted by someone who's never painted before.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'You don't look very happy in this.' Johnny: 'It wasn't the style to look happy. The motivation was to look successful.'
jokes.setup: Stevie and Johnny examine the portrait together
jokes.punchline: Johnny's earnest explanation that looking happy was not the artistic brief — 'successful' was the goal, implying these are mutually exclusive
jokes.explanation: This line carries both comedy and pathos — it's funny as a social observation (wealthy people posing for portraits without smiling), but also reveals something genuine about how the Roses lived. The implicit suggestion that happiness and success were separate pursuits adds depth without laboring the point.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Her eyes are following me.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that was an expensive upgrade.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Well, you're together like all the time now. So who needs this, right?'
jokes.setup: Johnny has just delivered a touching speech about how the portrait reminded him the family was together
jokes.punchline: Stevie punctures the sentiment with cheerful practical logic: you're all crammed in a motel, so the portrait is redundant
jokes.explanation: Stevie's functional pragmatism deflating Johnny's nostalgia is a recurring dynamic that works well. The 'so who needs this, right?' is said without malice, making it funnier — she's genuinely trying to be helpful.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Can Gwen bring a plus-one?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Who's Gwen?

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Really? You'd think there'd be more of a market for oversized paintings of other people's families.'
jokes.setup: Johnny says the portrait is one of the only possessions not sold off
jokes.punchline: Stevie's faux-surprise that the market for 'oversized paintings of other people's families' is limited
jokes.explanation: Stevie at her dry best. The joke works on multiple levels: it's a genuine observation (who WOULD buy this?), it gently mocks Johnny's nostalgia for his possessions, and the phrase 'oversized paintings of other people's families' is perfectly clunky in a way that makes it funnier. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: How many people are coming?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, Twyla's bringing her whole family.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Her whole family?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Yeah, apparently they're quite large.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Why does it seem like Mrs. Rose's eyes are following me?' Johnny: 'Oh, she paid extra for that.'
jokes.setup: Stevie notices the portrait's eyes seem to follow her around the room
jokes.punchline: Johnny's casual revelation: Moira specifically paid the artist extra for that effect
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best jokes in the episode. The setup (eyes following) is a familiar artistic trope; the punchline (Moira paid extra for it) is the perfect Moira-specific twist. It tells us everything about Moira Rose in six words: she had the portrait commissioned with eyes that haunt everyone in the room, intentionally, as an upgrade. Johnny's breezy matter-of-factness about it makes it even better. Extremely quotable, high rewatch value, exceptional character integration. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor — this clears it.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, the guest list has really... expanded.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's gotten completely out of hand.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I mean, at this point we're just... inserting people everywhere.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm, and now it's starting to feel a bit too tight.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Perhaps we need to pull back and think about the... depth of what we're doing here.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I think we're in too deep at this point.

jokes.text: Twyla brings tuna melts that weren't ordered: 'I didn't order tuna melts, but I don't have time...'
jokes.setup: David sits down at the café; Twyla brings food
jokes.punchline: The food is wrong but David has already given up caring — 'I don't have time' implies resignation to Twyla's chaos
jokes.explanation: Functional Twyla-as-chaotic-server joke. The humor is in David's resigned acceptance more than any specific wit. Sets up the information-leak joke that follows.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Can I help you find something?

dialogue.speaker: Unknown customer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm looking for a gift for my cousin's roommate's boyfriend's sister.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Right. And what's your budget?

dialogue.speaker: Unknown customer
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not trying to spend a lot since I've never met her, but I also don't want to be rude.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That makes sense.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown customer
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? Forget it. This is too much pressure. I'm just going to show up empty-handed and be aggressive about it.

jokes.text: Twyla has told people about the 'big store opening' — David corrects: 'which is why you would be invited as a friend'
jokes.setup: David has been trying to keep the soft launch exclusive and small
jokes.punchline: Twyla has already told people about it, describing it as a 'big store opening' — the opposite of what David intended
jokes.explanation: A necessary plot beat dressed up as comedy. David's correction about why Twyla would be 'invited as a friend' has a mild ironic sting but isn't particularly sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: This is unacceptable! Your service is terrible!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to have to ask you to lower your voice and watch your language in my store.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Or what? You'll what, exactly?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I... I'll be very disappointed. In you. And possibly... ask you to leave.

jokes.text: Roland appears: asks if both he and Gwen get plus-ones OR have to be each other's plus-ones. David: 'Who's Gwen?'
jokes.setup: Roland shows up asking detailed questions about the invitation policy for him and 'Gwen'
jokes.punchline: David has no idea who Gwen is — Roland has apparently invited strangers who David has never heard of
jokes.explanation: Roland's bureaucratic concern about plus-one logistics for an event he was never meant to be at is very on-brand. 'Who's Gwen?' lands well as a simple exposure of how far this has already spiraled.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This is exactly what happens when you take business advice from Gwyneth Paltrow. You end up with too many people in your store and a vague sense that you should be doing something with jade.

jokes.text: Patrick reveals Patrick told 'everyone on the pre-approved list' — it's spread to Twyla's whole family: 75 people
jokes.setup: David confronts Patrick about how word has spread; Patrick defends himself by saying he only told the pre-approved list
jokes.punchline: The pre-approved list apparently includes Twyla's 'whole family' — 75 people — demonstrating the futility of a 'soft' launch
jokes.explanation: The '75' is the perfect number — big enough to be a disaster, specific enough to be funny. Patrick's explanation ('when you tell everyone it's exclusive, then everybody just wants in') is a good observational beat about exclusivity marketing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David's store is absolutely wonderful. The displays are impeccable, the selection is curated, and the customer service is... familial.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Of course, I would only shop there out of family obligation, but that's beside the point.

jokes.text: The 'soft/firm/hard launch' sexual innuendo exchange between David and Patrick
jokes.setup: Patrick jokes that with this many people the soft launch is 'firming up'; David responds 'as long as it doesn't get hard'
jokes.punchline: David immediately realizes what he said — 'And that's something, that's what I just said to you, so...' — followed by a beat of silence
jokes.explanation: The joke works because the double entendre is entirely accidental — David walks into it and immediately clocks it. The 'that's what I just said to you, so...' is the perfect embarrassed acknowledgment. Their relationship subtext makes the innuendo funnier for rewatchers. High impact for a live audience.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Can I use this foot cream on my hands?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's... it's foot cream.

jokes.text: David is confronted in line by 'Darleen's cousin' who tells him to go to the back of the line at his own store: 'Who the fuck are you?'
jokes.setup: David approaches the line outside his own store and tries to assert himself
jokes.punchline: A stranger he's never met tells him — at his own store — to go to the back of the line and doesn't know who he is
jokes.explanation: The inversion of ownership — David being locked out of his own event — is a classic comic premise executed cleanly. 'Who the fuck are you?' lands especially hard because it's directed at the store's owner.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: David, what exactly is in this tea?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's just a blend of herbs and botanicals. Why?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Because you're being very secretive about it, and you've suddenly got all this money, and people keep coming by the store asking for it in hushed tones.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Those are just satisfied customers, Jocelyn.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: David, I think you're dealing drugs.

jokes.text: David's response to being cursed at: 'That kind of language, folks, will not be tolerated at Rose Apothecary. Thank you. This is a safe place.'
jokes.setup: David has just been told to fuck off at his own store opening
jokes.punchline: Rather than engaging in conflict, David pivots to brand management — declaring Rose Apothecary a 'safe place' to the gathered crowd
jokes.explanation: Pure David Rose. Rather than defending himself as a person, he defends his brand. 'This is a safe place' delivered to a chaotic mob outside his own store is a perfect encapsulation of his values hierarchy: aesthetics and brand above personal dignity. The formality of 'Thank you' mid-chaos is the cherry on top. Justifies 8+ through exceptional character integration and quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, you know, I like to enjoy a nice cup of tea in the evening. Really helps me relax. Gets me nice and loose.

jokes.text: David describing being 'verbally assaulted by a very off-brand customer'
jokes.setup: David reports the confrontation outside to Patrick
jokes.punchline: His characterization: not a rude customer, not a threatening stranger — an 'off-brand customer,' filtered entirely through aesthetic/brand language
jokes.explanation: 'Off-brand customer' is a perfect David Rose coinage — it translates a threatening human encounter entirely into marketing vocabulary. His priorities are clear: the issue isn't personal safety, it's brand coherence. Very quotable and deeply character-specific. Callback to the safe-place moment.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I have some big news.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, are you finally going to tell me that what happened between us meant something?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: What? No. You got your GED.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I have really underestimated you... Who knew you had so many friends. Or family members, for that matter.'
jokes.setup: David reports chaos at the launch; Moira says she's underestimated him
jokes.punchline: Her 'compliment' is actually a backhanded insult about his small social circle — the 'or family members, for that matter' is the knife
jokes.explanation: Moira's backhanded compliments are a series staple. 'Or family members, for that matter' is the perfect Moira sting — implying she's surprised David has any connections at all while nominally complimenting him.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, darling, I just wanted to say how thrilled I am about your... engagement. You're going to make such a beautiful bribe— I mean, bride.

jokes.text: David realizes he never called the electrician; Patrick reveals he watched YouTube tutorials and wired the lights himself
jokes.setup: The store opening is chaos; David panics about the lights
jokes.punchline: Patrick has already handled it via YouTube tutorials — and mentions he also got the insurance David forgot to get, 'just in case' he wired it wrong
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Patrick got insurance specifically because he wasn't sure his YouTube wiring was safe is a great double-beat: first relief (Patrick handled it), then mild horror (there's a good chance it's wrong). Very Patrick — competent, prepared, and honest about his limitations.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We could cut it up into sections and hang them in different rooms.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, what if we just cut out your face and hang that?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, no.

jokes.text: David: 'Softly.' — as the doors to the soft launch are being opened
jokes.setup: Patrick says 'Open the doors'; there is a mob outside
jokes.punchline: David's single-word reply: 'Softly.' — desperately trying to maintain the soft-launch branding even as everything is out of control
jokes.explanation: One word encapsulates the entire episode's comedic throughline. David's insistence on 'Softly' as chaos reigns is a perfect callback to his soft-launch obsession. It works as both absurdist stubbornness and character comedy — he knows it's too late, but the branding must be honored. Perfect economy score. Justifies 8+ through economy, structure, and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I got you a gift for graduation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, thank you!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It's a pen.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: A pen.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You got me a pen for graduation.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It's a nice pen.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's a pen, Ted.

jokes.text: David: 'Softly.' as the instruction for opening the store doors
jokes.setup: The soft launch has become a big event; David is about to open the doors
jokes.punchline: His instruction to open 'softly' — clinging to the soft launch concept even as it's collapsed — is both funny and character-true
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — one word — that calls back the entire episode's A-plot in a character-specific way. David still wants his soft launch even as it's completely derailed. 'Softly' as the only instruction is a perfect comic button that earns the rewatch bonus for structural elegance.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: The pencil was expensive, and the pricing wasn't even clear on the package. It just said 'premium pencil' — premium what? Premium eraser? Premium wood? I'm standing in the office supply store for twenty minutes trying to figure out if I'm about to spend eight dollars on a pencil or eighty dollars.

jokes.text: Johnny and Moira visit the store and are genuinely moved: Moira says 'I would shop here, John. Even without a nagging sense of obligation.'
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira visit David's store and are impressed
jokes.punchline: Moira's highest compliment: she would shop here even if she weren't obligated to as his mother — the bar for maternal support is delightfully low
jokes.explanation: Moira's highest praise framed as 'even without obligation' is a beautiful character joke. It's simultaneously warm (she really means it as a compliment) and devastating (this is apparently the gold standard of maternal enthusiasm). Very quotable and Moira-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Okay, so we need to establish some ground rules here. No kissing before 9 AM on weekdays, no kissing after 10 PM on weeknights, no kissing in front of my colleagues or their families, no kissing in any public establishment, no kissing near the town well, and absolutely no kissing if either of us has recently consumed beets.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'The Rose name on another plucky young business.'
jokes.setup: Johnny sees the 'Rose Apothecary' labels and feels pride
jokes.punchline: 'Plucky young business' — an endearingly small-scale callback to Rose Video, as though an artisanal apothecary is in the same tradition
jokes.explanation: A warmly ironic line — Johnny treating Rose Apothecary with the same gravity as his multinational video empire. 'Plucky' is a great word choice that works in multiple directions.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, a 40% discount on opening day does tend to clear out inventory. I'd say we've successfully launched Rose Apothecary.

jokes.text: Customer's question: 'This says foot cream on it. What happens if I use it on my hand?' David: 'I'm pretty sure your hands are gonna be fine.'
jokes.setup: A customer holds up foot cream with what seems like a serious product question
jokes.punchline: David's barely-suppressed exasperation: 'I'm pretty sure your hands are gonna be fine' — a magnificent understatement for a question that barely needed asking
jokes.explanation: David's barely-civil retail persona is perfectly captured. The deadpan 'I'm pretty sure your hands are gonna be fine' is a restrained put-down that only works because of his visible effort to stay professional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, the eyes... they're following us.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that's because they're real eyes. Which, in retrospect, was not the best decision.

jokes.text: Roland asks if he and Jocelyn both buy foot cream simultaneously, does that count as a 50% discount — David: 'Have you been talking to my dad?'
jokes.setup: Roland tries to negotiate a stacked discount by combining his and Jocelyn's purchase
jokes.punchline: David immediately recognizes this as the same impossible discount logic his father proposed earlier
jokes.explanation: A tight callback that rewards attentive viewers. Roland independently arrives at exactly the same ridiculous discount logic as Johnny, and David's recognition of the pattern ('have you been talking to my dad?') is perfectly structured. The callback elevates both jokes and reveals that Johnny and Roland share a particular brand of transactional logic.

jokes.text: Jocelyn asks if the Joshua tree tea is drugs — 'Is it drugs?'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn picks up a 'baggy of Joshua tree tea' and asks about it with an air of knowing suspicion
jokes.punchline: 'Is it drugs?' — delivered completely seriously, as though this is a reasonable retail question
jokes.explanation: The directness of 'Is it drugs?' — no preamble, no apology — is the joke. Jocelyn's cheerful small-town pragmatism makes it funnier than if it were played as accusatory.

jokes.text: David explaining the tea came from 'Mr. Hockley who makes it out of a greenhouse on his farm... and now that I've said it out loud, I might have to double-check.'
jokes.setup: David defends the tea as legitimate loose-leaf tea from a local farmer
jokes.punchline: Saying his own explanation out loud makes him doubt it — 'greenhouse on his farm' does indeed sound like a drug operation
jokes.explanation: David's self-undermining realization — 'now that I've said it out loud, I might have to double-check' — is a great moment of self-awareness. The beat where the store owner questions whether his own product might be drugs is a perfect escalation of Jocelyn's question.

jokes.text: Moira and Johnny's ultra-vague cedar dragon tea discussion: 'I don't know. I mean, it's nice but... I think you should probably get it or not, whatever'
jokes.setup: Moira and Johnny taste a tea called 'Cedar Dragon' and discuss whether to buy it
jokes.punchline: Their review is spectacularly uncommitted — maximally vague while maintaining the air of having said something
jokes.explanation: A gentle moment of comedy that relies on the Roses' habit of speaking with authority while saying nothing. Mild laugh, not especially notable.

jokes.text: Ted mentions 'the big news' and Alexis panics, starts to explain 'the hug' — Ted clarifies he meant exam results
jokes.setup: Ted greets the Rose parents and says he 'takes it Alexis has told you the big news'
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately begins confessing about the kiss/hug; Ted interrupts — he meant the exams. Double embarrassment: she nearly self-incriminated AND has to receive congratulations for barely passing
jokes.explanation: A masterfully constructed comedy beat. It works on three simultaneous levels: (1) misdirection — the audience and Alexis both assume 'big news' means the kiss; (2) cringe — Alexis almost confesses in front of her parents; (3) callback — brings together the exam plot and the kiss subplot in a single moment. The structure is excellent: setup creates maximum expectation of embarrassment, punchline redirects to something innocuous but still slightly mortifying (her barely-passing grades). One of the episode's best constructed jokes.

jokes.text: Moira's garbled recovery: 'I had nothing but doubts... No doubts. That you could do this.'
jokes.setup: Moira reacts to hearing Alexis passed her exams and tries to say something encouraging
jokes.punchline: She starts to say she had 'nothing but doubts,' catches herself, reverses mid-sentence — producing something that technically says the opposite but sounds exactly as damning
jokes.explanation: This is a brilliant piece of character comedy. Moira tries to correct herself in real-time but only makes it worse — the grammatical reversal ('nothing but doubts... no doubts') is technically correct but emotionally identical. It's entirely Moira: theatrical, self-aware, and still accidentally honest about her low expectations. Justifies 8+ through exceptional character integration and structure.

jokes.text: Moira's verdict on the portrait: 'It doesn't fit, John. And I don't just mean literally.'
jokes.setup: Johnny suggests cutting their faces out of the portrait to make smaller individual portraits
jokes.punchline: Moira's more decisive solution: 'We bid it farewell.' Her observation that it doesn't fit 'literally' or figuratively lands as both funny and genuinely poignant
jokes.explanation: The portrait storyline gets its best line here — it's funny as a practical observation and carries genuine emotional weight about the Roses' changed circumstances. The double meaning is handled with elegance rather than over-explanation. Schitt's Creek at its characteristic blend of comedy and warmth.

jokes.text: Johnny suggests cutting their faces out of the portrait to make 'four smaller, individual portraits'
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira are trying to figure out what to do with the too-large portrait
jokes.punchline: Johnny's solution — a kind of portrait cannibalism — is both practical and horrifying in equal measure
jokes.explanation: A nicely absurd solution that has Johnny's pragmatic engineer character all over it. It's funny because it's both efficient and completely wrong as a solution. Solid mid-range joke.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Were we really that happy back then?' Moira: 'Yes.' Johnny: 'We were.' Moira: 'Oh, deliriously happy.' Johnny: 'I miss that beach house.'
jokes.setup: Johnny reflects philosophically on whether they were happy in their old life
jokes.punchline: The moment of genuine emotional reflection immediately deflates into nostalgia for the beach house — the spiritual becomes material in two lines
jokes.explanation: The pivot from 'were we truly happy' to 'I miss the beach house' is a perfectly Roses comic beat — they can hold a profound thought for approximately one exchange before wealth-nostalgia overtakes it. The warmth is genuine, the comedy is in the rapid descent to the material.

jokes.text: Ted's gift to Alexis: a pencil shaped like a twig from the store
jokes.setup: Ted has 'gotten Alexis something' for passing her exams — she reacts like it might be jewelry
jokes.punchline: It's a pencil shaped like a twig — from David's store, where they currently are — and it was 'a lot more expensive than I thought' because it had no price tag
jokes.explanation: A sweet visual gag that works on multiple levels: the absurdity of the gift, Alexis's genuine delight at something so small, and Ted's indignant complaint about unmarked prices being a trap. 'I hate it when they don't put price tags on things' is a great button — he spent real money on a decorative twig-pencil.

jokes.text: Ted gives Alexis 'a little something' for passing her exams — it's a twig. Ted: 'No, it's a pencil shaped like a twig. I got it at the store.'
jokes.setup: Ted has bought Alexis a gift from David's store to celebrate her exam success
jokes.punchline: The gift is a pencil shaped like a twig — from an apothecary store — which is both perfectly Ted and accidentally from a store that sells it as a serious item
jokes.explanation: The twig-pencil is a great object comedy choice — it's self-consciously artisanal in the way that Rose Apothecary products are, but when Ted presents it sincerely it reveals both his earnestness and the store's absurdities. Alexis's 'Oh, Ted. You picked up a stick' is equally perfect.

jokes.text: Ted: 'Yes. A lot more expensive than I thought too. I hate it when they don't put price tags on things.'
jokes.setup: Alexis notes the twig-pencil looks 'very delicate'
jokes.punchline: Ted's complaint about price tags in artisanal stores is both a real frustration and a commentary on the store's aesthetic choices — very relatable but also very Ted
jokes.explanation: A nice small observation that's character-true and relatable. Ted's mild grievance about price-tag-less artisanal stores is exactly the kind of thing a sincere, non-trendy person would notice. Not exceptional but well-placed.

jokes.text: Alexis's accidental kiss — meant as 'a thank-you adult kiss' — followed by Ted's mortified 'I think maybe we should just avoid kisses for a while. Even adult ones.'
jokes.setup: Alexis thanks Ted for the gift and kisses him again by accident or instinct
jokes.punchline: Ted's pained, layered response banning all types of kisses — 'even adult ones' — with the callback to their earlier rationalization
jokes.explanation: The callback to the 'adult kiss' rationalization pays off when Alexis deploys it again sincerely and Ted shuts it down completely. The escalation to 'adult hug' and 'adult work together' and 'adult high five' is a perfect extended button.

jokes.text: 'Adult high five' — Ted and Alexis's final resolution of the adult-kiss situation
jokes.setup: Ted has banned all kisses including 'adult ones' and proposed 'adult hugging' and 'adult working together' instead
jokes.punchline: Alexis offers an 'adult high five' — the taxonomy of 'adult [mundane action]' has become absurd — and they both do it
jokes.explanation: The 'adult high five' is a perfect culmination of the running 'adult [action]' gag. The joke has escalated from 'adult kiss' to 'adult hug' to 'adult high five' — the diminishing intimacy is the joke, and the high five is the perfect comic landing point. Economy score reflects maximum comedy from a two-word phrase.

jokes.text: Alexis proposes an 'adult high five' as their final resolution
jokes.setup: They've been trying to establish what physical contact is appropriate
jokes.punchline: 'Adult high five' — the most infantile possible gesture given the 'adult' prefix treatment — is the logical endpoint of the running gag
jokes.explanation: The perfect button to the running gag. 'Adult high five' collapses the logic — a high five is the least adult-coded gesture possible, making the prefix absurd. It's well-structured as a closer and highly quotable. One of the episode's better small jokes.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'Although, you know, we'd be 25 percent richer if we'd just done a hard launch. But hey, I'm just a numbers guy.'
jokes.setup: The launch has been a success; David and Patrick debrief
jokes.punchline: Patrick revives the hard/soft launch debate to needle David one final time, with the disclaimer 'I'm just a numbers guy'
jokes.explanation: Patrick's victory lap is understated and perfectly delivered. 'I'm just a numbers guy' is a great fake-humble button — he's absolutely right and knows it. Good callback to close the commercial subplot.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'I can fix that.' — immediately after the store's success, something in the store breaks/is wrong
jokes.setup: The partners are celebrating success in the immediate aftermath of the launch
jokes.punchline: Something immediately goes wrong and Patrick's calm 'I can fix that' suggests it's something he's aware of — possibly the questionable YouTube electrical work
jokes.explanation: A functional callback to the electrical wiring concern. Patrick's calm response suggests this was always expected — a good button for that earlier joke. Not exceptional but tidy.

jokes.text: The portrait finds its way to the motel front desk; Johnny: 'I wonder what else the Van Housens took from us.'
jokes.setup: The portrait, which they decided to 'bid farewell,' has now been rehung — apparently at the motel
jokes.punchline: Johnny's wistful musing about other stolen possessions — which misses the point that they CHOSE to display it here
jokes.explanation: A mild callback to the Van Houstens and the portrait's origin. The portrait persisting into the final scene is a good structural choice but the specific line isn't the sharpest in the episode.

jokes.text: Alexis defends the portrait: 'Speak for yourself, I look super cute.' Johnny: 'Okay, that's not actually your body though.' Alexis: 'Okay, but it's my face, David.'
jokes.setup: Moira suggests they bid the portrait farewell because 'we aren't those people anymore'
jokes.punchline: Alexis objects on the grounds that she looks cute — despite the fact that it's not her body. She then calls Johnny 'David' by mistake.
jokes.explanation: A superb final callback to the episode's best joke. Alexis defends her inclusion in the portrait ('I look super cute') while Johnny reminds her it's not her body — she proudly owned someone else's body all these years. Then she calls her father 'David,' which lands as a perfect unscripted-feeling button. Everything about this is character-specific: Alexis's vanity, her casual acceptance of the body-swap, and her cheerful confusion about family members' names. Exceptional character integration and structure. Among the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: David: 'There's something really weird happening with Mom's eyes. It's like they're following me.' Johnny/others: 'Oh, they are.' David: 'Oh God.'
jokes.setup: The portrait is now at the motel; David notices the eyes seem to follow him
jokes.punchline: Johnny confirms it — the eyes do follow. Callback to the earlier 'she paid extra for that' revelation.
jokes.explanation: The episode's final joke is a tight callback to one of its best setups. David discovering the portrait's following eyes and being told they DO follow — confirmed — is a perfect closing beat. It brings Moira's vanity joke full circle and ends the episode on the portrait's lingering comedic presence. 'Oh God' is the ideal final word.

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Moira struggle to find a place for their family portrait. David hosts a 'soft launch' of his new store.
insight: Bittersweet character comedy sustains 47 jokes across emotional family reckoning.
